name: Plutonium
name_origin: Named after the dwarf planet Pluto, continuing the sequence of element names following uranium and neptunium.

discovery.discoverer: Glenn T. Seaborg, Edwin McMillan, Joseph W. Kennedy, Arthur Wahl

occurrence.abundance_summary: Found in trace amounts in natural uranium ores from neutron capture on U-238. The vast majority of plutonium is produced artificially in nuclear reactors.

compounds.name: Plutonium dioxide
compounds.summary: Yellow-brown ceramic solid; the form used in mixed-oxide (MOX) nuclear fuel and as the heat source in radioisotope thermoelectric generators using Pu-238.

compounds.name: Plutonium tetrafluoride
compounds.summary: Pale brown crystalline solid; intermediate in the production of metallic plutonium by reduction with calcium.

compounds.name: Plutonium trichloride
compounds.summary: Green crystalline solid used in molten-salt electrorefining of plutonium metal.

compounds.name: Plutonium(IV) nitrate
compounds.summary: Soluble compound used in the PUREX process to separate plutonium from spent nuclear fuel.

uses: Fissile material in nuclear weapons (using Pu-239)
Radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs) for spacecraft using Pu-238 (Voyager, Cassini, Curiosity, Perseverance, New Horizons)
Heat source in radioisotope heater units for spacecraft
reactivity_summary: A silvery metal that quickly tarnishes to a dull olive color in air, forming an oxide layer. Plutonium has six allotropes at ambient pressure and unusual physical properties including density that varies with temperature. It reacts with most acids and shows complex chemistry with multiple oxidation states (commonly +3 and +4) coexisting in solution.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Plutonium-244 has the longest half-life (80 million years). Pu-239 (half-life 24,110 years) is the principal fissile isotope used in weapons and reactor fuel. Pu-238 (half-life 87.7 years) is the heat source in RTGs. Pu-240, Pu-241, and Pu-242 are all of nuclear-engineering interest.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
